bibliographic_data.title: Negative Examples for Sequential Importance Sampling of Binary Contingency Tables
bibliographic_data.abstract: The sequential importance sampling (SIS) algorithm has gained considerable popularity for its empirical success. One of its noted applications is to the binary contingency tables problem, an important problem in statistics, where the goal is to estimate the number of 0/1 matrices with prescribed row and column sums. We give a family of examples in which the SIS procedure, if run for any subexponential number of trials, will underestimate the number of tables by an exponential factor. This result holds for any of the usual design choices in the SIS algorithm, namely the ordering of the columns and rows. These are apparently the first theoretical results on the efficiency of the SIS algorithm for binary contingency tables. Finally, we present experimental evidence that the SIS algorithm is efficient for row and column sums that are regular. Our work is a first step in determining the class of inputs for which SIS is effective.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper provides theoretical results showing that the Sequential Importance Sampling (SIS) algorithm can grossly underestimate the number of binary contingency tables by an exponential factor for certain inputs, even when run for a subexponential number of trials.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors construct specific examples where SIS fails to provide accurate estimates and analyze the behavior of SIS on these examples.
outcomes: SIS can underestimate the number of tables by an exponential factor
Experimental evidence suggests SIS is efficient for regular row and column sums
core_content.full_text_markdown:
# Negative Examples for Sequential Importance Sampling of Binary Contingency Tables

## Abstract
The sequential importance sampling (SIS) algorithm has gained considerable popularity for its empirical success. One of its noted applications is to the binary contingency tables problem, an important problem in statistics, where the goal is to estimate the number of 0/1 matrices with prescribed row and column sums. We give a family of examples in which the SIS procedure, if run for any subexponential number of trials, will underestimate the number of tables by an exponential factor. This result holds for any of the usual design choices in the SIS algorithm, namely the ordering of the columns and rows. These are apparently the first theoretical results on the efficiency of the SIS algorithm for binary contingency tables. Finally, we present experimental evidence that the SIS algorithm is efficient for row and column sums that are regular. Our work is a first step in determining the class of inputs for which SIS is effective.

## Introduction
Our focus is on algorithms for estimating |Φ|, where Φ = Φr,c denotes the set of m × n 0/1 tables with row sums r and column sums c. There are algorithms [11, 3] for estimating |Φ| (and sampling almost uniformly at random from Φ) which provably run in time polynomial in n and m for any row/column sums. We discuss these algorithms, which use Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods, in more detail later in the introduction. In this paper, we study a simpler method known as sequential importance sampling (SIS).

SIS has several purported advantages over the more classical MCMC method, such as speed and convergence diagnostic. However, the authors present a simple example where SIS requires an exponentially large number of samples to give an approximately correct answer.

## Methodology
The general importance sampling paradigm involves sampling from an 'easy' distribution µ over Φ that is ideally close to the uniform distribution. At every round, the algorithm outputs a table T along with µ(T). Since for any µ whose support is Φ one has E[1/µ(T)] = |Φ|, the algorithm takes many trials and outputs the average of 1/µ(T) as an estimate of |Φ|. The sequential importance sampling algorithm of Chen et al. [7] constructs the table T in a column-by-column manner.

## Results
The authors construct specific examples where SIS fails to provide accurate estimates, showing that for certain inputs, SIS can underestimate the number of tables by an exponential factor even when run for a subexponential number of trials.
